questions.question: Explain the three requirements that must be satisfied by a solution to the Critical Section Problem.
keyPoints: MUTUAL EXCLUSION: Only one process can be in critical section at a time
PROGRESS: If no process is in critical section and others wish to enter, selection must be made without undue delay
BOUNDED WAITING: After a process requests entry, there is a limit on how many times other processes can enter before this process gets admission

questions.question: What is Peterson's solution for two processes? Explain with the shared variables used.
keyPoints: Uses two shared variables: 'turn' and 'flag[2]'
flag[i] indicates whether process i wants to enter critical section
turn indicates whose turn it is to enter
Process i sets flag[i]=true and turn=j, then waits while flag[j]=true and turn=j
Provides software-based mutual exclusion without hardware instructions

questions.question: Explain the difference between Binary Semaphore and Counting Semaphore with suitable examples.
keyPoints: BINARY SEMAPHORE: Takes only values 0 and 1, used for mutual exclusion (like a lock)
COUNTING SEMAPHORE: Can take integer values greater than 1, represents number of available identical resources
Example of counting: A printer pool with 5 printers - semaphore initialized to 5
Binary semaphore example: Protecting a shared data structure - initialized to 1
Binary semaphore ensures only one process accesses critical section at a time

questions.question: Describe the Producer-Consumer problem and explain how semaphores can be used to solve it.
keyPoints: Producer creates items and places in bounded buffer, Consumer removes items
Problems: Buffer overflow (when full) and buffer underflow (when empty)
Solution uses: mutex (binary) for mutual exclusion, full (counting) for filled slots, empty (counting) for empty slots
Producer: wait(empty), wait(mutex), add item, signal(mutex), signal(full)
Consumer: wait(full), wait(mutex), remove item, signal(mutex), signal(empty)

questions.question: What is the Readers-Writers Problem? Differentiate between Reader-Preferred and Writer-Preferred solutions.
keyPoints: Readers-Writers deals with access to shared data where multiple readers can read simultaneously but writers need exclusive access
READER-PREFERRED: Writers may starve as new readers keep arriving; readcount tracks active readers; writer waits only when readcount > 0
WRITER-PREFERRED: Writers get priority; writer waits if any reader is active or waiting; readers wait if a writer is waiting
Solution uses: readcount (number of readers), mutex (protect readcount), writelock (for writers)

questions.question: Explain the Test-and-Set instruction for implementing mutual exclusion. What are its advantages and disadvantages?
keyPoints: TestAndSet is a hardware instruction that atomically reads and modifies a memory location
It returns the old value while setting the location to a new value (typically true)
Implementation: A while loop repeatedly calls TestAndSet on a lock variable until it returns false
ADVANTAGES: Simple to implement, works in multiprocessor systems, provides mutual exclusion
DISADVANTAGES: Causes busy-waiting (spinlock), wastes CPU cycles, can lead to priority inversion

questions.question: Explain the Dining Philosophers problem. Discuss how deadlock can be avoided in this problem.
keyPoints: Five philosophers sit at round table, each alternates between thinking and eating, five chopsticks between them
Each philosopher needs two chopsticks to eat, can only pick up adjacent chopsticks
DEADLOCK OCCURS when all philosophers pick up left chopstick simultaneously, waiting forever for right chopstick
DEADLOCK AVOIDANCE SOLUTIONS:
1. ODD-EVEN STRATEGY: Odd philosophers pick left first, even pick right first
2. MAXIMUM N-1 PHILOSOPHERS: Allow only 4 philosophers at table
3. ASYMMETRIC SOLUTION: Some pick left, others pick right
4. CENTRALIZED CONTROL: Pickup and drop requests go through arbiter
5. BROKEN CIRCULAR WAIT: Release left chopstick if right unavailable

questions.question: What are semaphores? Explain the implementation of wait() and signal() operations. Also discuss the difference between strong and weak semaphores.
keyPoints: Semaphore is an integer variable with two atomic operations: wait() and signal()
wait(S): If S <= 0, block the process; else decrement S
signal(S): Increment S; if there are waiting processes, wake one up
IMPLEMENTATION: Use a queue to store blocked processes; signal wakes one from queue
STRONG SEMAPHORE: FIFO order of wakeup - guarantees bounded waiting (no starvation)
WEAK SEMAPHORE: No guarantee of order - may lead to starvation
Binary semaphores used for mutual exclusion, counting semaphores for resource management

questions.question: Discuss the classical synchronization problems: Producer-Consumer, Readers-Writers, and Dining Philosophers. Explain how each can be solved using semaphores.
keyPoints: PRODUCER-CONSUMER: Bounded buffer problem - use mutex, full, empty semaphores
READERS-WRITERS: Multiple readers allowed, writers need exclusive access - use readcount, mutex, writelock
DINING PHILOSOPHERS: Resource allocation with 5 philosophers and 5 forks - use semaphore per fork or centralized control
Each problem illustrates different synchronization challenges: buffer management, concurrent read access, and deadlock avoidance
Solutions demonstrate use of binary and counting semaphores, proper ordering of wait() and signal() operations

questions.question: Explain the Critical Section Problem and discuss Peterson's solution in detail with a flow diagram.
keyPoints: CRITICAL SECTION: Code segment accessing shared variables that must not be concurrently accessed
THREE REQUIREMENTS: Mutual exclusion, progress, bounded waiting
PETERSON'S SOLUTION for 2 processes:
Uses shared variables: turn (whose turn), flag[2] (desire to enter)
Algorithm: Process i sets flag[i]=TRUE, turn=j; then waits while flag[j]=TRUE AND turn=j
Ensures mutual exclusion: Both cannot satisfy while condition simultaneously
Satisfies progress: If j doesn't want to enter, i proceeds
Satisfies bounded waiting: After j exits, i can enter
Limitation: Assumes atomic access to shared variables, only works for 2 processes
